div.h2: The CAS is the Single Instant Where Operations Take Effect

g.text: The CAS Window: Where Races Happen

div.p: Key Insight: Linearizability means each operation appears to occur atomically at its linearization point. All correct lock-free algorithms have well-defined linearization points — typically the successful CAS.
Theorem: The Treiber stack is linearizable. Linearization Points:

ul.li: pop(): Successful CAS on top Key Properties:

ol.li: Atomicity: Each CAS is atomic at hardware level
Total Order: CAS operations on same address are totally ordered

Real-time Preservation: If op A completes before op B starts, A's linearization point precedes B's Valid Linearization: ⟨ push_A(42), push_B(17), pop_C() ⟩ Result: pop_C returns 17 (top of stack after both pushes)

div.p: Sequential Specification: A stack is linearizable if there exists a total order of operations that:

ol.li: Satisfies LIFO semantics For our example:

ul.li: B's push(17) starts before t₁, completes after t₂
C's pop() starts after t₁, completes at t₃ Linearization: ⟨ A.push(42), B.push(17), C.pop()=17 ⟩ ✓ Valid: pop returns 17 (most recently pushed) ✓ Real-time preserved: A before C

text.tspan: // ← Other threads can modify top here
// ← Other threads can modify top here

ul.li: Any number of other pushes/pops can complete
CAS will fail (version mismatch) At CAS success:
Operation takes effect globally No partial states visible:
Stack is always in consistent state
